12: ouk estin en allo oudeni he soteria (ouk estin en allō oudeni hē sōtēria): 'in no other is there salvation' — Peter's exclusive soteriological claim before the Sanhedrin. onoma (onoma) carries Semitic weight: the name is the person, their character and authority concentrated. hypo ton ouranon ('under heaven'): the spatial comprehensiveness marks this as a universal claim, not a parochial Jewish sectarian position. The statement is the more remarkable for being made to the very council that approved Jesus's execution.
32: kardia kai psyche mia (kardia kai psychē mia, 'one heart and soul'): a Hellenistic friendship ideal (Aristotle's 'one soul in two bodies') now applied to the entire community. Luke's vocabulary of voluntary property-sharing (koina, all things in common; 2:44) differs structurally from the Qumran model where members transferred property to the community permanently. Jerusalem believers retained ownership (idios) and voluntarily brought proceeds as needs arose (5:4 confirms private property was not abolished).
1: strategos tou hierou (stratēgos tou hierou, 'captain of the temple') was the second-ranking authority in Jerusalem after the high priest, responsible for temple security and public order. His appearance alongside priests and Sadducees signals the full weight of the Jerusalem establishment mobilizing against the apostles; this is not a minor local altercation but an institutional response.
2: kataggellein en to Iesou ten anastasin ('proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection from the dead'): the preposition en is significant — the resurrection is proclaimed in Jesus, using him as the concrete instance that validates the resurrection reality. The Sadducees denied resurrection on principle; the apostles were not preaching a general philosophical resurrection but pointing to a specific man who had risen and thereby demonstrated the doctrine in historical fact.
3: eis teresin (eis tērēsin, 'into custody'): a technical legal term for preventive detention pending investigation, distinct from punitive imprisonment. Luke's precision — 'because it was already evening' — notes that the timing prevented immediate trial; Sanhedrin procedure required daylight hearings. The detail is historically credible and marks Luke's familiarity with Jewish juridical practice.
4: arithmos ton andron ('the number of the men'): Luke counts men (andrōn) specifically, following Jewish convention that tallied males for quorum and membership purposes. The number 5,000 represents a significant fraction of Jerusalem's population; the community has grown from 120 (1:15) to 3,000 (2:41) to 5,000 in rapid succession. Luke structures his growth notices as theological progress reports, not merely sociological data.
5: archontes kai presbyteroi kai grammateis ('rulers and elders and scribes'): the three constituent groups of the Sanhedrin, the supreme Jewish council of 71 members that held judicial, legislative, and administrative authority. Luke uses this tripartite formula deliberately — the identical body that condemned Jesus is now summoned against his apostles. The narrative parallelism is intentional: the disciples face the same tribunal that their Lord faced.
6: Annas had been deposed as high priest by the Romans in AD 15 but retained enormous personal authority and the loyalty of the priestly aristocracy; his sons and son-in-law Caiaphas (still serving as high priest, cf. Luke 3:2; John 18:13) constituted a dynastic priestly faction. Luke's list of names signals historical specificity and suggests access to accurate sources about Jerusalem's power structure in the 30s.
7: en poia dynamei e en poio onomati ('by what power or by what name'): the two questions are functionally equivalent in Jewish thought — the shem (name) was the concentrated reality and authority of a person, so action performed 'in a name' was action powered by that name's authority. The Sanhedrin wants to know whose delegated power the apostles have invoked, assuming some authorized intermediary. Peter's answer (v.10) uses precisely this framework but names a name they did not authorize.
8: plesthe Pneumatos Hagiou (plēstheis Pneumatos Hagiou, 'filled with the Holy Spirit'): Luke's aorist passive marks this as a fresh, specific endowment for this occasion, not simply the ongoing condition of 2:4. Peter before the Sanhedrin enacts the promise of Luke 12:11-12 ('when they bring you before the synagogues and the rulers and the authorities, do not be anxious about how you should defend yourself... the Holy Spirit will teach you in that very hour what you ought to say'). The fulfillment is immediate and precise.
9: euergesia (euergesia, 'good deed/act of benefaction'): Peter uses the standard Hellenistic vocabulary of public benefaction — the euergetēs (benefactor) was a patron whose good deeds benefited the city. By framing the healing as a euergesia, Peter subtly argues within the council's own civic framework: they are prosecuting a case of public benefit, not public harm. Luke's Greek shows Peter adapting register to his audience.
10: en tō onomati Iēsou Christou tou Nazōraiou ('in the name of Jesus Christ the Nazarene'): the full messianic title with geographic qualifier. Nazōraios in Acts (2:22; 3:6; 4:10; 6:14; 22:8; 24:5; 26:9) maintains continuity with the ministry of Jesus of Nazareth against any tendency to abstract the risen Christ from his historical particularity. The name is simultaneously claim, invocation, and challenge: the council crucified this man, and God raised him.
11: ho lithos ho exouthenetheis ... eis kephalēn gōnias (Ps 118:22): Peter applies the psalm directly to the council. Kephalē gōnias ('head of corner') is disputed — either the capstone completing an arch or the massive foundation stone at the base of a corner. Either meaning emphasizes indispensability: the stone the builders discarded is the one on which the whole structure depends. The quotation reframes the crucifixion as a builder's error that God corrected by resurrection-exaltation.
13: agrammatos kai idiōtai ('unlettered and untrained'): agrammatos means lacking formal scribal education — the ability to read, interpret, and teach Torah in the scribal tradition. Idiōtēs is a layperson relative to a professional; in rhetorical contexts it means an untrained speaker vs a trained orator. The council's astonishment is that these men argue with force and coherence despite lacking the credentials that would normally explain such performance. Their only explanation is the training of Jesus (v.13b).
14: theōrountes (theōrountes, 'seeing/beholding') is Luke's vocabulary for significant observation — more than mere glancing. The healed man's physical presence as a living exhibit makes refutation impossible; any argument the council might construct against the healing's reality is undercut by the 40-year-old man standing with his healed limbs on display. Luke notes the council's rhetorical problem: evidence stood in front of them.
15: The Sanhedrin's private deliberation (keleusantes autous exō tou synedriou apelthein) is a narrative concession to realism: Luke acknowledges he cannot have attended. The detail suggests either that a participant later disclosed the discussion (a sympathizer like Nicodemus or the Gamaliel of ch.5) or that the substance of the decision became known from its public outcome. Luke does not claim omniscience; he reports what became known.
16: semeion gnōston ('notable sign'): the council's internal framing is revealing — they accept the sign's factual reality (gnōston, 'known/evident') but treat it as a political problem rather than a theological datum. This is the pattern Luke identifies as culpable unbelief: those with sufficient evidence who choose political calculation over truth. Their question 'what shall we do?' (v.16) echoes the crowd's question in 2:37, but leads to suppression rather than repentance.
17: apeilē (apeilē, 'threat') + me phthenggesthai ('not to utter'): phthenggesthai is a significant word — used in LXX for prophetic utterance (Mic 5:12; Ezek 13:9; Ps 19:3) and by Luke in 2:4 and 2:14 for inspired Pentecost speech. The council's command to stop phthenggesthai is unknowingly a command to cease prophetic proclamation. Luke's word choice signals that the apostles' speech has a higher category — suppressing it is suppressing prophecy.
18: kathōlos ('at all/entirely'): the absolute prohibition — not merely reduced teaching but a complete ban on speaking or teaching in this name. The council's overreach establishes the conflict of loyalties that v.19-20 will address. The command is total, which is why Peter's answer (dei, 'it is necessary') must be equally total: divine necessity overrides human prohibition.
19: ei dikaion estin ('whether it is just'): Peter invokes the council's own standards. In Greek political philosophy and Jewish legal tradition, obedience to unjust commands was not obligatory. Peter does not refuse outright but poses the logical prior question — is this command lawful? He then appeals to God's court, leaving the evaluation to the audience. The argument has Socratic precedent (Plato, Apology 29D) and Jewish martyr tradition (2 Macc 7).
20: ou dynametha ('we are not able'): the phrase carries both epistemic force (we cannot deny what we have seen) and volitional force (we cannot choose to keep silent about it). The apostles do not merely say 'we will not' but 'we cannot' — their witness is constrained by what they have directly seen and heard (ha eidamen kai ēkousamen). Eyewitness testimony is their irreducible ground; it cannot be surrendered by command.
21: kolasosin (kolasōsin, 'to punish/chastise'): the council wanted to punish the apostles but was constrained by public opinion (ton laon). Luke's narrative repeatedly notes this popular-political check on official action (cf. 5:26; Luke 20:6; 22:2). Doxazon ('glorifying') — the people's response to the healing is praise for God, precisely what the council cannot argue against without alienating the population they need.
22: Luke's parenthetical note that the healed man was over forty years old functions as evidentiary detail: the duration of the disability eliminates the possibility of spontaneous healing or misdiagnosis. The Sanhedrin cannot argue statistical coincidence against a man congenitally lame from birth (3:2) for over four decades. Luke's attention to ages throughout Acts (the man here; Paul in various passages) reflects Hellenistic historical writing's use of concrete detail to establish credibility.
23: tous idious ('their own people'): the phrase signals communal identity — the apostles' primary accountability is to their own community rather than the council. Luke's use of idios in Acts (2:6; 4:23, 32; 24:24; 25:19) consistently marks belonging. The community gathers to hear the report and turns immediately to prayer, modeling the pattern of apostolic community: shared experience leads to corporate prayer leads to renewed mission.
24: Despota (Despota, 'Sovereign Master'): less frequent than Kyrios, this address emphasizes absolute sovereignty and ownership — the relationship between a master and his household. The prayer opens by anchoring God's authority in creation (citing Ps 146:6 / Exod 20:11) before petitioning him to act in history. The logic is implicit: if you made all things, you govern all things; therefore the council's opposition is within your sovereign management.
25: pais (pais, 'servant/child') is applied to David (v.25) and Jesus (v.27, 30). The word bridges the Davidic covenant and the Servant Songs — it can mean both 'son' (in the sense of Ps 2) and 'servant' (in the sense of Isa 52-53). Luke's consistent application of pais to Jesus (3:13, 26; 4:27, 30) is his preferred Servant-Christology term in these early speeches, keeping the dual resonance of royal son and suffering servant in view simultaneously.
26: The community's prayer cites Ps 2:1-2 (parestathesan hoi basileis tēs gēs kai hoi archontes synēchthēsan epi to auto kata tou kyriou kai kata tou christou autou) and then immediately identifies its historical referent in vv.27-28. This is the apostolic hermeneutic in concentrated form: take a psalm, identify its referent as Christ, then read the psalm's terms as the grid for reading history. Herod and Pilate are the 'kings and rulers' of Ps 2; the Gentiles and Israel are the nations; the conspiracy was the Passion.
27: aleipsas (hōn echrisen, 'whom you anointed'): the verb chriō (to anoint) links Jesus directly to the title Christos (the Anointed One, Messiah). The anointing referred to may be the Spirit's descent at baptism (Luke 3:22; 4:18 — 'the Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he has anointed me'), the incarnation itself, or the resurrection-exaltation. The prayer assumes that Ps 2's royal anointing has been applied to Jesus, making every subsequent opposition to Jesus a fulfillment of the psalm's scenario.
28: proōrisen (proōrisen, 'foreordained/predetermined'): Luke's strongest statement of divine determinism in Acts, alongside 2:23 ('delivered up by the definite plan and foreknowledge of God'). The council's decision, Pilate's capitulation, Herod's involvement — all were 'predetermined by your hand and your counsel' (tē sē boulē). This is not fatalism that removes human responsibility (the actors are still culpable) but providence that guarantees divine purpose through human free action. The tension is held without resolution, as in the OT wisdom tradition.
29: meta parresias pases (meta parrēsias pasēs, 'with all boldness'): parrēsia (boldness, freedom of speech) is a key term in Acts (2:29; 4:13, 29, 31; 9:27, 28; 13:46; 14:3; 18:26; 19:8; 26:26; 28:31). In Hellenistic political philosophy it was the freedom of a citizen to speak freely before the assembly; in Acts it is the Spirit-given freedom to speak the gospel before any audience, regardless of threat. The community does not pray for deliverance from opposition but for boldness to continue speaking within it.
30: en tō ekteinein se ten cheira sou ('as you stretch out your hand'): the divine hand as agent of miraculous power is OT idiom (Exod 3:20; 9:15; Deut 26:8; Ps 138:7). The prayer frames the apostles' healing ministry as God's own hand working through them — they are instruments, not autonomous agents. sēmeia kai terata ('signs and wonders') is the standard OT theophany-vocabulary (Exod 7:3; Deut 4:34; 6:22; 7:19; Ps 78:43) now applied to apostolic ministry.
31: esaleuthē ho topos ('the place was shaken'): physical phenomena marking divine response to prayer echoes OT theophanies (Exod 19:18 — Sinai shaking at the divine descent; Isa 6:4 — the temple threshold shaking at the divine glory). The community is refilled with the Spirit (eplēsthēsan hapantes) — Luke does not treat Pentecost as a once-for-all endowment never needing renewal. The Spirit is continuously given in fresh measure for fresh occasions of witness.
33: megale charis en epi pantas autous ('great grace was upon them all'): charis in Luke-Acts carries both divine favor (God's gracious disposition) and the concrete expression of that favor in community life. The phrase functions as a summary theological assessment of the community: their unity, generosity, and witness are the marks of divine charis resting on them. The construction echoes similar summaries in Luke's Gospel (Luke 2:40 of the child Jesus — 'the grace of God was upon him').
34: ouk en endees tis en autois ('there was not a needy person among them'): the exact fulfillment of Deut 15:4 LXX ('there shall be no poor among you') — a striking echo that Luke almost certainly intends. The Deuteronomic ideal of a community without poverty, which Israel never fully realized in the land, is enacted in the Jerusalem church. Luke presents the community's economic life as the actualization of covenantal promise.
35: para tous podas ton apostolōn ('at the feet of the apostles'): the gesture of placing something at someone's feet is a posture of voluntary submission and recognition of authority. The Twelve receive and administer the common fund — not by compulsion but by communal agreement. The prepositional phrase establishes apostolic authority as the structural center of the community's economic life, a detail that prepares for the crisis of ch.5 and the administrative expansion of ch.6.
36: pais paraklēseōs (huios paraklēseōs, 'son of encouragement'): the Semitic bar- construction (son of X = characterized by X) indicates that Barnabas was known as an encourager — one who exhorted, consoled, and strengthened others. Paraklēsis is the same word-family as paraklētos (the Advocate/Spirit, John 14:16); here it identifies a human ministry of comfort and exhortation. Luke introduces Barnabas here because he will become Paul's first significant partner in mission (9:27; 11:22-30; 13-15).
37: The Barnabas episode (hyparchontos autō agrou, 'having a field') functions as the positive exemplar that immediately precedes the Ananias and Sapphira counter-example of ch.5. Luke's narrative pairs the genuine and the counterfeit: Barnabas's wholehearted surrender of the proceeds is the model; Ananias and Sapphira's deceptive partial surrender is the perversion. The field/land vocabulary links both narratives, making the contrast structural.
